comments.title: Priority of Daily Work, Twice Daily Meditation, Middle Pillar vs Inner Grail, Elemental "Powers"
comments.content:
Hello!

As always, thanks very much for your time and care in answering these questions.

1. For this one, I know that the right answer is "reorganize your life and your priorities so you can give you daily magical work the time and attention it deserves". That being said, if one does decide to spend less time on magic on a given day, is there an order of priority as to what to do? Is it better to do a shorter version of all three core practices (ritual, meditation, divination), or to pick one or two (I assume ritual and meditation are more vital to do than divination)?

2. Another poster asked on the practical upper limits of meditation, and you recommended no more than two 15 minute sessions, one in the morning, and one in the evening. Does it make a difference if these are on similar or very different topics? For example, Debby the dabbler might meditate on the Ogham in the morning and a tarot major arcana in the evening, whereas Bob the eager beaver might choose to meditate on two different correspondences for one of the fews in the same day. Would you expect these two to have comparable or rather different outcomes, assuming both are already fairly comfortable with meditation and good at setting aside the needed time and following through?

3. It seems that the Middle Pillar exercise and the Inner Grail exercise are roughly equivalent, in terms of having the point of awakening centers in the subtle body/bodies, and yet the MP is introduced very early in GD practice, whereas the Inner Grail is not introduced until rather later in the DMH. Is this because the use of two currents, rather than one, is more challenging/risky, or for some other reason?

4. Perhaps a minor point, but in the Sphere of Protection, for each element we call on the "powers" of that element - does this mean more "strengths, abilities, potentials", or more "spiritual beings of unspecified kind/rank/strength", or is the ambiguity perhaps intentional?

Thanks,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Priority of Daily Work, Twice Daily Meditation, Middle Pillar vs Inner Grail, Elemental "Powers"
replies.content:
1) Reorganize your life and your priorities so you can give you daily magical work the time and attention it deserves. That really is the only correct answer.

2) It varies from person to person.

3) The Middle Pillar wasn't traditionally introduced early in GD practice. In the original order, you didn't learn it until you reached Portal grade -- roughly two years after you start work. That's longer than necessary, but it's good to put in a few months of hard work before you get to it. The same is true of the Grail working -- and since I have students start that with self-initiation rituals, they need to have the ritual framework down before they do that.

4) The ambiguity is entirely intentional.

replies.title: Re: Priority of Daily Work, Twice Daily Meditation, Middle Pillar vs Inner Grail, Elemental "Powers"
replies.content: I suspected the answers to 1, 2, and 4, but 3 was news to me, thanks very much!

replies.title: Re: Priority of Daily Work, Twice Daily Meditation, Middle Pillar vs Inner Grail, Elemental "Powers"
replies.content: 2. For the last year I have been doing my meditation sessions with about 17 minutes total. About 2 minutes to relax, then roughly 5 minutes of colored breathing, and then 10 minutes on a theme. I was thinking of going further to increase the time spent on a theme to 15 or further down the road to 20 minutes. Is this advisable? Should I consider the relaxation and breathing also part of the meditation time, or just the main part?

replies.content: Most people can stretch it to 20 minutes without the least difficulty, and quite a few can take it to 30. I'm referring here to the time actually spent working on the theme.

comments.title: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
comments.content: I've been reflecting on a lot the past few weeks.  One of the things that keeps recurring to me is the loss of several dear family members over the past few years: my father, an aunt, two uncles, a brother-in-law, and others.  Is it possible and advisable to create a shrine to one or more of them, and perhaps make offerings and seek some kind of contact with them?  I would especially like to do this with my father and my brother-in-law, but I am not sure if this is wise.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: Unless you belong to a tradition that practices ancestor reverence, I recommend praying to the deity of your choice instead, and asking the deity to bless the dead people. Contact between the living and the dead is not generally a good idea.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: That implies that going to a medium to contact your dead relatives is not a good idea.  Even if the medium was legit?

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: Most occult schools consider passive trance mediumship to be a bad idea, partly because it has very negative effects on the medium, but partly because you can never be sure what kind of spirits will actually show up, and some of them can be malign entities. And of course a great many mediums are fraudulent!

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content:
I assume "passive trance mediumship" means you let something else take over your body? What negative effects does it have on the medium? I imagine it would be negative if a malign entity took over but doesn't mediumship training mitigate that risk?

Is it also negative on the medium if it is "drawing down the moon" or embodying gods? Or is the risk of something nastier getting in still a possibility?

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content:
1) Passive trance mediumship leaves you in a negative condition, vulnerable to whatever entity happens to be strolling around. Active mediumship -- when you remain conscious the whole time -- is a different matter and is much safer.

2) It depends very much on the quality and effectiveness of the protections that are used to prevent trouble. Vodoun and other African diaspora religions have been doing this for a very long time with good results, for example, but then they've got the magical chops to do it safely.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: Would you say the method that was used in The Gate of Remembrance to be active or passive mediumship?

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: I'd have to reread the book to reacquaint myself with the technique. If the medium is conscious through the process, it's active mediumship.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content:
What sort of problems could contact cause, for the living or the dead?

I assume the protection a tradition would bring would be well established safe guards in place to prevent problems from occurring (correct me if I'm wrong), but what about the more modern movements that don't have, or even see the need for, such protections? I was recently looking over the internet presence of one of the modern polytheistic groups that has ancestor worship (possibly as a requirement? at least for organization membership?), and found myself idly wondering what sort of trouble one could invite or cause by treating the afterlife as another place to recruit a team of personal spiritual allies to "work with."

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: My guess is that they're going to end up with a lot of people suffering from obsession.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: I suppose I'm correct in my assumption the majority of them, if indeed they are in contact with anything, it's not the spirit of their long deceased relatives? What are they likely contacting, do you think?

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: There's a whole gallimaufry of low-grade spirits who can pretend to be Aunt Mabel, and it's hard to tell what's present in any given case.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: What do you think then about the procedure described by Levi, about three-quarters of the way through the book, to receive a vision of a dead loved one? (You're not going to be getting to that chapter in your reading group for close to three years, and my spouse died this year ... I'm impatient.)

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: Lévi recommends it, I don't. Take your pick.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: Why do occultists think contacting the dead is a bad idea?

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: The same reason it's unhealthy to spend a lot of time around dead things on the physical plane: you can catch things that are bad for you.

replies.title: Re: Ancestor Veneration and Contact
replies.content: That’s logical.

comments.title: Guidence on the path of the lonely ones

1) You might ask the Ogham! If I had to guess, however, it's because these days a lot of groups are very troubled and toxic.

2) It can be very challenging to work with an inner plane guide, and a lot of people who try it stop making their own decisions and expect the inner plane guide to do that for them. This isn't at all helpful. Give it time; when you're ready, you'll make contact.

3) I do what you do -- trace it in front of me and then send it down beneath me.

2) That sounds like plenty of work to me!

comments.title: Filling in the blanks for a lunar conjuration
comments.content:
Hi John
I'm consecrating a lunar talisman tomorrow, but there's one detail I haven't been able to figure out for the ceremony. How exactly should I fill in the blanks of the conjuration example you give in the simple consecration ritual in Circles? I'm guessing the intelligence of intelligences goes in "planetary intelligence", but I'm not sure who should I call where the conjuration says "planetary spirit". Is it the spirit of spirits, or is it Chashmodai? Whats the difference between them?

Thanks as always
JP

replies.title: Re: Filling in the blanks for a lunar conjuration
replies.content: Yes, you invoke the Intelligence of Intelligences in the space for the planetary intelligence, and you invoke both the spirit of spirits and Chashmodai under planetary spirits. Chashmodai is the specific spirit, but Shad Barshemoth ha-Shartathan  is the spirit who rules all lunar spirits, so you call on him and then Chashmodai. (The Moon is so much closer to Earth that more of its hierarchy interacts with us.)

replies.title: Re: Filling in the blanks for a lunar conjuration
replies.content: Ah, so that's what the addendum refers to: you say "spirits of levanah, shad barshemoth ha sharthathan and chashmodai". I thought it applied just to Yesod workings.

replies.title: Re: A question about a loaded topic
replies.content:
Perfect timing.
(I second what the other commentor said about the Nuremburg Code, by the way. The coronavirus vaccines are legally "experimental therapy".)

This weekend I dug into the VAERS database.

VAERS is a venue where people-- doctors and non-doctors both-- can *report* what they feel are adverse reactions to a vaccine. The 'events' in the database are just that-- reports; not confirmations that the vaccine truly caused whatever reaction is reported.
So, VAERS can be read as a sense of the public's reaction to getting this, that or the other vaccine.

I looked at the timeframe 2019, 2020, and 2021.
I compared (1) all other vaccines combined, to (2) just the coronavirus vaccines.
The VAERS data were current up to 16 July 2021 when I did my digging.

Overall summary of reports ('events'):

All Other Vaccines Combined:
Death - 69
Life Threatening - 275
Permanent Disability - 618
Hospitalized - 1,159
Emergency Room - 4,681

Coronavirus vaccines only:
Death - 6,304
Life Threatening - 7,375
Permanent Disability - 6,314
Hospitalized - 25,829
Emergency Room - 57,733

VAERS database search page: https://wonder.cdc.gov/vaers.html
How To use the search page: https://www.cdc.gov/vaccinesafety/ensuringsafety/monitoring/vaers/access-VAERS-data.html

A given search only returns so many results, so I had to break down all of the searches into age groups. A lot of the coronavirus vaccine searches I had to further break down into blocks of states for each category, then add the blocks back together to get those totals. It took hours.

- -

In addition, to the point about people getting one shot but not the second:
https://www.mayoclinic.org/coronavirus-covid-19/vaccine-tracker/,
"U.S. COVID-19 vaccines over time"

Look at the "Fully vaccinated" percent for any date, then the "at least one dose" percentage for 4 weeks before that date. If everyone who gets the first shot gets the second one 4 weeks later, these percentages should match. They don't. As of today's date, approximately 16 million Americans have gotten one shot but not the other yet. I can't tell yet whether they are ever going to or not (the numbers are still moving up, but slowly.)

Anecdotally, I have one coworker who got the first jab, had diarrhea for a week, and swore he would not get the second shot.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: A question about a loaded topic
replies.content:
The placebo effect is a real thing, as is its lethal twin, the nocebo effect. There is a fair amount of scientific literature on them in medical journals, and occasionally also in anthropological journals. In each case, an inert pill or injection, or a well-designed ritual, will produce the same results as an actual medicine or an actual poison. Our host is quite right that the effectiveness of the placebo or nocebo is not a matter of some opinion you have decided to hold, but draws on a person's subliminal consciousness. Mere "thinking" is not enough to make something so by itself.

With enough skill and understanding, one can even activate the placebo effect in oneself, though it takes a great deal of effort. (I have actually had to do so once or twice in somewhat unusual circumstances.)

That very strange and somewhat demon-haunted magician, William B. Seabrook, devoted a chapter or two in his book, Witchcraft, Its Power in the World Today (1940) to activating these effects from an experiential point of view (not a scientific one). Half a century later, the forensic psychologist who wrote Sorcery under the pseudonym "J. Finlay Hurley" approached the same problems from a more scientific point of view.

Since I am learning about the occult, I see occult themes everywhere.

For instance, I recently completed a RPG game named Persona 4. This game is rooted in Jungian philosophy and it does not shy about it, there is even a channel in the game with a teacher talking about Jung. The game itself is a bit dull and I had to force myself to finish it just to build up some diligence. In this game, you have to know (as in gnosis) a few people, all shaped by tarot cards. For example, the Hanged is the story of a boy who has lost his sister and hasn't been able to cry for her yet. You progress in his story faster by saying what the boy wants to hear so he trusts you and opens himself to you (that is, don't talk about the dead sister before he is ready). This seems to be a very japanese thing, the art of talking wihtout offending.
Characters go to another dimension by watching an unplugged TV at night, in a way that resembles scrying, but they don't explicitly say so. There, inside the TV, they find the shadows, made out of the desires of people. Characters that face their own shadows, and accept that these shadows are a part of them, become stronger and are able to command their Personas. (Know thyself?)
The final battle is against the goddess that created Japan in mythology and the weapon that defeats her is the Orb of Truth, made from the fragments of memories with the power to dispel any fabrications and lies. After being defeated, she admits to have touched three newcomers to the village with the power to contact their shadows, and I couldn't avoid to think about traveling atoms that attract other established atoms in their orbit. These three characters were foreigners (traveling atoms) but only one of them managed to gather enough villagers (static atoms) in his orbit and defeated the goddess (created a body/mind?).
Nothing of this was explicit. Whoever designed the game knew this stuff very well.

In another TV show (The Irregulars, set in the Sherlock Holmes scenery, but it's about magic), the Magus of a mystical order called Golden Dawn was found dead in the shape of the Mage tarot card. The inner circle is then gathered to choose a new Magus. One of the attendants is called Alan Crowley, portrayed as an egotistic fanatic who believe in magic, but without any powers himself. Spoiler, he was not the assassin.

In an outreach channel called Veritasium, I learned about the E. Coli long term evolution experiment, by Richard Lenski. That was really interesting. At one point, the bacteria developed the ability to feed on a different substance in the growth medium. Since they can 'rewind', they went back to previous samples to find that the bacteria developed the new ability again and again, but not always at the same time. The theory says that when all permutations have been tested, a new genotype arises, but the exact moment is ruled by randomness. It was a personal shock to find the exact same words of the evolution of a Cosmos upon Fortune in the mouth of a reputable scientist. It was, in some sort, the confirmation that genes have memory.

I've also meditated a little about what I learned when I meditate on the visions from the scrying. A metameditation. They are almost always subsconscious thoughts expressed in images. These thoughts I could have thought them in words, which is my usual way. In fact, I don't make much sense of them until I meditate on them and translate the images to words.
It struck me that what I am doing is learning a new language, made out of images instead of words. I am letting my mind express itself in images and learning what they mean in the process. Nature doesn't talk with words. It manifests itself to our senses, but what we get is the raw images and sounds. It is actually saying things, but we can't learn what it is saying since we don't know the language.
If I put them into words, I'm doing a translation of a translation, and thus I am missing much of the message.

Watching a movie with my son is very revealing. Many scenes are full of implicit messages that I can make sense because I have experienced the situations, while my son who is completely fresh is clueless. That leads to him laughing at some scenes and me laughing at completely different ones. But I can't show him what was funny, he just doesn't understand. He needs to experience things and then come back so the message is seen again in a new light. What applies to him, applies to me I guess. I remember well when you said that occult is not actually hidden, the knowledge is there in plain sight if you know what you are looking at. It's just the same reason why my son doesn't get the jokes for adults. I have to experience more things and be attentive if I want to increase my understanding, to attain sageness.

PS. On the subject of translations, how can you possibly translate a calembour? The most famous is from Quevedo: "Entre una flor y una rosa, su majestad escoja", which the artist said to the lame queen of Spain while offering her some roses, who hated being mocked of her lameness. Any attempt at translating will either ruin the joke or the message.

comments.title: Best argument against reductive materialism?
comments.content: Dear Archdruid, I wanted to ask you what the best argument is against the position that all magical, mental and spiritual phenomena are just some type of neurological hallucination. I do not sympathize with the intentions of the people who believe in this type of reductive materialism, but I am a cynical person by nature and find that argument to be deeply seductive and resonant with the cynical parts of my own mind.

replies.title: Re: Best argument against reductive materialism?
replies.content: The same logic used by reductive materialists can also be used to prove that reductive materialists themselves are just some type of neurological hallucination. The logical fallacy at the heart of materialism is the claim that experiences of the kind we call "sensory" are by definition objectively real and external to the self, while all other experiences are by definition neither. That claim can't be defended rationally -- any good survey of epistemology will introduce you to the reasons why it falls flat on its nose.

replies.title: Re: Best argument against reductive materialism?
replies.content:
Reminds me of the classic Douglas Adams quote

The argument goes something like this: "I refuse to prove that I exist,'" says God, "for proof denies faith, and without faith I am nothing."
"But," says Man, "The Babel fish is a dead giveaway, isn't it? It could not have evolved by chance. It proves you exist, and so therefore, by your own arguments, you don't. QED."

"Oh dear," says God, "I hadn't thought of that," and promptly vanishes in a puff of logic.

"Oh, that was easy," says Man, and for an encore goes on to prove that black is white and gets himself killed on the next zebra crossing.”

comments.title: planetary aspects
comments.content: I had a conversation with someone who mentioned they didn't have a good relationship with a certain deity because that deity's planetary aspect in their natal chart wasn't good.  Have you found that planetary aspects are indicative of a relationships with and/or between deities?  My interest specifically is with Mercury, the Moon, and the Sun.  I've been working with Hermes since the Intro to High Magic and it has been going very well.  For Litha, I honored Apollo and Artemis (and that continues to go well, too).  And then after the comment about planetary aspects, I looked them up on my natal chart and found that Mercury and the Moon are combust (both within 4 degrees of the Sun).  Considering their relationship as deities in Greek mythology and then considering their relationship in the symbol for Azoth (and considering some of the insights they have provided me), it is intriguing to say the least.  But I've not found very clear information about what exactly a combust aspect means.  (The Sun absorbs the characteristics?  The Sun 'burns off' the characteristics?  And then different sources have different degrees of what combust is?)  (Or maybe it means that the deities just like hanging out with each other...)

replies.title: Re: planetary aspects
replies.content: Planetary aspects can be reflected in relations between deities, but the converse is also true. The fact that you've had fine results from invoking Hermes, Apollo, and Artemis makes it very clear that your natal chart isn't hindering things -- so don't worry about it.

replies.title: Re: planetary aspects
replies.content:
Combustion means that the planet is so close to the sun that it isn't visible to the eye (15 degrees is the number I use). So the planet's effects are obscured or burned and the indication is that that planet's characteristics will be more hidden in the person's life. For example, you will often see a very quiet or soft-spoken person with Mercury combust.

If you look at the cycle of inner planets as they relate to the sun, they are first visible as evening stars, then go combust and are invisible. When they are exactly lined up with the sun (a conjunction within less than a degree - here again sources vary but some require it to be as little as 15 minutes) they are called cazimi. The planet has entered the heart of the sun and has been purified and reborn. After that they are combust again as they travel out from the sun and finally they rise to the eye as morning stars. It makes quite a nice topic for meditation and you can see where some of the indications in a chart come from. If you want to look into it more, search for "cazimi" and "heliacal rising/heliacal setting."

Combustion with the Moon is a little bit of a special case since a combust Moon is a New Moon (or a very late balsamic moon) so you have some of the same invisibility and rebirth signification but the Moon has its own whole body of interpretation and lore and such.

-Breanna

comments.content:

There has been several posts here about the magical dimensions of the old latin ritual of the catholic mass and its subsequent replacement by a new mass which appeared to be much less efficacious in bringing down energies.

replies.title: Re: The Catholic Church, Magic and religion
replies.content:
Former catholic here. These are all really good questions, but also ones that involve things that the church really doesn't like to talk about. But as a catholic turned occultist:

1) The church HATES having any of its actions understood in light of a separate framework. Formal doctrine divides existence into material and spiritual, there were more graduated models used in the past but they have long since fallen out of favor. Using Greer's scheme, the effect seems to be primarily on the higher planes (mental) with a weaker but pervasive astral effect. Effects on the etheric plane seem to depend on both the celebrant and the context of the mass.

2) The original mass was created by skilled ritualists who knew what they were doing and then practiced it thoroughly and systematically for centuries, building up a huge amount of force in the process. The new mass was created by people who don't seem to know what makes rituals work and modern teaching on that point seems to be immensely confused. All this is aggravated by point 3.

3) The Council of Trent brought the full spiritual authority of the church down in support of the idea that the mass WILL be in Latin, and proclaimed an anathema against anyone who says otherwise. It is entirely possible that when the second vatican council tried to convert the mass into vernacular, they accidentally excommunicated themselves.

4/5) I can't say why this happened. It is an excellent question but also one that falls well outside the purview of us mere mortals. The church does indeed have powerful enemies, and it is also quite fashionable to blame them for such things. The uncomfortable truth of the matter though is that the Roman church's greatest adversary is most likely itself. During the reformation, the church's response to widespread corruption was to double down in the spiritual elements that caused the reformation in the first place, and they only cracked down on corruption in the church only insofar as it weakened papal authority. The same process, transposed to the key of post-modernism, appears to be taking place now.

6) It comes up from time to time, but is rarely discussed so openly or as the topic of conversation. Beyond that, its deeply fashionable in the church to deny that there is any kind of problem. When I was in school (catholic education) the brothers were complaining about the newly re-translated English mass that the students (including myself) picked up on it. It not working right was a definite subtext of these conversations, but never explicitly stated. I also know that some exorcists have very publicly complained that the new rite of exorcism doesn't work so they went back to the (no longer ordinary) old one.

replies.title: Re: The Catholic Church, Magic and religion

replies.title: Re: The Catholic Church, Magic and religion
replies.content:
1) The goal of a ritual such as the Mass is to bring down positive influences from the highest, spiritual planes so that they come within reach of worshippers who can't yet reach those planes themselves. Properly done, those energies set up an influence that goes all the way down the planes to the material plane -- thus the focus on material objects such as bread and wine at the center of the rite.

2) Because it was designed by people who didn't know the first thing about the inner side of ritual, and were obsessed (possibly in more than one sense) with the idea of making it fit a series of fashionable intellectual hobby horses. You don't get good ritual that way.

3) Because most of the generation of Catholic leaders who established the new rite and continue to maintain it embraced one or another variant of Modernist-influenced theology, and thus had no clue about the spiritual and magical dynamics of good ritual. I've been told by former Catholics that a lot of priests and bishops of that generation actively hate the old Latin Mass -- I suspect it's because the spiritual forces at work in the ceremony make them very uncomfortable.

4) I don't know for certain, of course, but my guess is that this is simply another example of what happens when you let bureaucrats with no active spiritual life run your church. Such people tend to be hostile to actual spirituality because it's a power they can't control.

5) Nothing good. If the hierarchy retains its deathgrip, the Catholic church will go the way of the big (formerly) mainstream Protestant denominations, which embraced modernism and are in steep decline because they offer no spiritual sustenance to their congregations. The alternative is schism, as I don't think the Modernists will accept a return to taking the spiritual dimension seriously.

6) It's discussed all over the traditionalist Catholic end of the internet. Here's one example and here's another.

comments.title: Tarot and Tricksters
comments.content:
Thanks, as always, JMG, for your generosity in helping others to wade through the often murky subject of the occult. This week I seek your views on a couple of subjects:

1 – lately I have been reading A.E. Waite’s The Pictorial Key to the Tarot (since I am studying his deck in depth right now) and have been flabbergasted by his cavalier comments at the end of his interpretation of the meanings of the cards. For the major arcana, he describes the meanings as “at once artificial and arbitrary”, and “the allocation of a fortune-telling aspect to these cards is the story of a prolonged impertinence”; and for the minor arcana, “It will be observed that these additamenta have little connexion with the pictorial signs of the cards to which they refer” and he is critical of both the “standard” meanings of the cards and the intuitive meanings derived from individuals. I also note that the “meanings” given are about as deep as the canned meanings used by the Golden Dawn for geomancy (which are disgraceful). It appears to me that Waite designed these cards strictly for the purpose of training the imagination and sneered at their use for divination. Am I on the right track?

2 – I have been noting the representation of trickster figures in popular North American culture over the past century… its ebb and flow and twists and turns. Bugs Bunny was a chaotic and somewhat malevolent figure when he started out in the 1930s (just ask Elmer Fudd!), but after WW2, under the direction of Chuck Jones there was a golden rule that Bugs only plays tricks on a person after they first have played a trick on Bugs or on another innocent victim. I’d say after 1980 or so, Bugs Bunny faded away with the advent of absolutely ghastly and ham-fisted (not to mention primitively illustrated) weekend cartoons on the TV. And then out of the blue came The Mask (1994) which was a brilliant burst of Loki-ness with the lesson that the magic mercurial mask would reflect the motive of the person who wears it. And in the 21st century we have Marvel’s version of Loki who was initially cast as a villain, but in the new TV series is trying to cast him as a hero (but oddly enough, he isn’t much of a trickster; rather he is faced with a more powerful malevolent universal trickster – I suspect that in Season 2, he will emerge as an heroic trickster… but we’ll have to wait and see). So, the Trickster is back in popular culture… but is he the bad guy?... the good guy?... can we ever tell? There must be a deeper reason for the often-dark Loki figure to appear in our culture again and I suspect that in times of transition (like what we are facing right now) that is a good thing. I know you don’t watch movies or TV, but from your somewhat distanced perspective, what do you see in all this?

Many thanks in advance for your responses.

Gamboge Abominable Prairie Dog

replies.title: Re: "A daily magical working..."
replies.content:
If you are like me regarding divination, then I think you can safely try scrying. There's a completely rational explanation why this works:
Usually we don't let our subconscious speak (you know, that part of the brains that belong to the animal), we repress it because we don't like it or whatever, the result is that we don't really know ourselves. By scrying, we let the subconscious express itself. By writing down and meditating on the images we can understand what this part of us is thinking on.

Sometimes our subconscious is aware of things that we miss and it will give you knowledge of your environment. Sometimes it is just being egotistic so you can grasp how other people see your inner you, this is very useful for understanding how you actually are. Know thyself!: If you want to move from point A to B, you have to know where point A is.
I've read that ultimately you can get images from the astral plane, not just from yourself, but I haven't reached that state, and honestly, so far it has been a worthy experience.

A few tips I've learned so far:
- Never try scrying in a sleepy state or in a too comfy position. Images will turn into dreams and you will not remember them. Sleeping well seems to be very important to any magical practice.
- Don't make it too long. You have to write in your journal what you have seen, but there's a limit on how many images you can memorise. My optimal is around 6 images. I see many more, but then I can only remember those.
- It helps a lot to perform a ritual like the Middle Pillar in advance. Feeling spiritually refreshened gives better results.
- I need at least 15 minutes of complete isolation so I can calm, clear the mind, focus and let images come. Any interruption forces me to start from the beginning. Using an alarm is counterproductive.
- You will most likely be thinking about topics that happened very recently, and any symbols wil be made from things you already know. If you meditate on them, their meaning should be clear. No need to go check what this or that symbol usually means.
- Did I say you have to write the images in a journal? Just a little description of what you saw and you will remember. Meditation on what you wrote in the journal is fruitful, meditation on only the stuff you remember in your head is less so. It's a good idea to use something to take notes just after the scrying session.
- The technique for making images arise is up to you. What works best for me is just closed eyes, focus on breath for a while and then let it go. No critics, no analysing, only ackowleging what I am seeing and trying to remember (You may see very scary things, but they are not scary when you aren't analysing them). Looking at myself in a mirror also works, but it limits the scope of the visions on only stuff about myself. Shinny surfaces didn't work very well for me. Water didn't work at all. Just try different things, note it in the journal and learn what gives better results.

These mistakes, while serious, are hardly irreparable. Mercury has likely had his fun. A gentler and more respectful approach, and simply asking if he is willing to continue the relationship, would probably be the way ahead. Mercury is, among other things, a god of communication; so why don't you try to communicate!

- A somewhat less savoury topic, but in the course of reading about the Roman gods, I came across Priapus, and found a mention that the Greeks, Romans and Egyptians all appeared to view semen as a somewhat toxic substance. A little off-topic (and off-colour...), but do you have any idea why this was?

replies.title: toxicity of seminal fluid
replies.content:
Many cultures have taboos around sexual fluids--seminal, menstrual, the products of childbirth, etc. These taboos are frequently interpreted by modern investigators as meaning that said products are seen as toxic, dirty, polluting, etc. In particular they have been interpreted as meaning that women are unclean by nature and that the pollution is acquired by intimate contact. However others see these taboos as a result of extreme respect for the life force. Mana, is a term from the South seas cultures that has been extended in usage for this type of discussion. Some Polynesian groups never had intercourse in their dwellings, but rather retired to the bushes. Of course the dwellings were not very private, so that could be a motive as well. The Hebrews were another group that felt this way about seminal fluid-- a man was "ritually unclean" and forbidden to enter the Temple or touch certain sacred objects after intercourse of any emission of semen. In the early Christian church women did not return to church until ritually 'churched" after giving birth. And so on. There are folklore beliefs along this line as well, such as the persistent rumor that menstruating women attract bears. In short, interpreting certain beliefs and practices is complex, especially when the reports of the beliefs and practices come through people who don't understand or approve.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Interaction of Magic/Belief Systems
replies.content: This notion that belief is necessary for magic to work is very widespread these days and very inaccurate. Magic is magic; it doesn't work on the conscious level of the mind, the level where beliefs hang out -- it works at deep levels which are untouched by your conscious opinions. I've seen magic work very well against people who didn't believe in it at all, so my working guess is that competent magical methods could be used against skinwalkers. The question would be figuring out, in Western terms, how their magic works, so that the right sort of countermeasures could be directed against it.

replies.title: Re: Interaction of Magic/Belief Systems
replies.content: What our host said!  People of little or no belief can still work magic, sometimes quite effectively.  You don't need to have any particular beliefs about an axe to chop wood with it quite effectively.  If fact, usually wood-chopping goes much more smoothly if you aren't asking yourself whether the axe and the wood are "real."

comments.title: Parasites, spiritual and biological
comments.content:
OK, my old biological-sciences background had me look quite a bit into behavioral aspects of organisms. A virus is in effect a parasite. There are quite a number of behavior-altering parasites in all bio Kingdoms. See: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behavior-altering_parasite for a discussion.

The key about such things: it’s Evolution In Action. The parasite does not work towards modifying the behavior of its host; no, it’s just selected for such effects if they happen to appear.

Both Covid-19 infection and the spike-protein-breeding “vaccines” result in a certain worrying number of associated neurological changes. Sooner or later a virus was bound to come along that affected human mental health, affecting it in such a way that the virus survived and flourished.

Creating a “vaccine” that does NOT prevent infection but which lessens the likelihood of the hosts dying is EXACTLY what any virus would benefit from. Hmmm.

If exposure to the Covid-19 spike protein causes you to behave less rationally, and that advances the likelihood of the Covid-19 virus continuing to reproduce, well, this seems like a plausible and useful-to-the-virus mechanism to me.

Apologies to all. Biology is messy. But on the positive side, life is very adaptable. Some members of a population will be more susceptible to any particular disease strain, and some less so. Again, Evolution In Action.

Here is a widely-quoted paper that discusses how the spike protein gets through the blood-brain barrier: https://neurosciencenews.com/covid-19-brain-protein-17458/

This SciAm article discusses quite a few of the neurological ramifications: https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/covid-can-cause-forgetfulness-psychosis-mania-or-a-stutter/

So since this is Magic Monday: oh ever-curious Archdruid, have you pondered on the occult connections, if any, between the various Realms and viruses? Very interested to hear your brief thoughts. Disclaimer: I have meditated on this topic quite a bit and prayed to Thoth to give me skill in writing, though I’m reluctant to share what I’ve (so far) concluded as I am such a tyro at Magic and occult practices… but I do wonder which spirits, Gods, or other beings would benefit and which would be opposed. Cui bono?

Dear JMG,

I have a few questions about the SOP/ Dolmen Arch:

1. Can the SOP be performed in a linguistically stripped-down form more akin to the LBRP? I've been doing it as written for 3 years and I feel like my focus and imagination could be more powerful with fewer words (plus I've internalised the words quite far at this point). Or are there reasons not to do this?

2. Can one invoke an alternate pantheon, such as Norse, in the SOP while studying the Dolmen Arch?

3. Is there any reason not to do SOP-based workings (as in the DMH) while primarily focussed on the Dolmen Arch?

4. What does it mean when one's feet feel very hot during the SOP? (if others experience this, I'd be interested to hear your takes too)

Thank you for Magic Mondays and all that you do!
Bewilderness

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1-2) While you're working with the specific DA system, keep the words exactly as given. Once you've finished the course you can start tinkering.

3) The DMH is wholly compatible with the Dolmen Arch -- the two can be practiced together.

4) You're getting plenty of flow through the foot centers from the telluric current.

comments.title: SOP Spirit Below Gate
comments.content: As it happens, most of the places I am able to perform the SOP currently are on the 2nd story or higher. When visualizing moving the Spirit Below gate to below my feet, would you recommend moving it down to ground level, or just to the level of the surface I’m standing on?

replies.title: Re: SOP Spirit Below Gate
replies.content: The level of the floor you're standing on is fine. It's below you, and that's what matters.

Hi JMG,

I have a few geomancy readings I would like to cross-check with you if possible.

1. I had a few interviews for a job, but it keeps getting delayed with the hiring manager going on holiday, other team members going on holiday etc. I was hesitant to ask this straight out at first but today after my daily practice I finally decided to ask:

Will I get this job?

Four Mothers:
Cauda Draconis
Via
Cauda Draconis
Albus


I decided to go with the Shield Chart primarily.

Court:
RW: Puer
LW: Puella
Judge: Conjunctio

Reconciler: Laetitia

Based on the court, I'd say it's quite positive. I might need to be a bit more proactive in reaching out to them, but I'll get it in the end? Laetitia as the Reconciler also sounds like a good sign, I'll like the job when I get it.

I didn't read the House Chart closely but I noticed that Cauda Draconis is very active in the chart.

2. I had a dream about a girl I used to date, I haven't really been in contact with her, although I do think of her. We had a very strong emotional/psychic bond right at the beginning when we first met, but I felt like maybe it had weakened towards the end. This dream was quite vivid, so I wondered if she had been thinking of me recently.

Is S. thinking of me?

Four Mothers:
Acquisitio
Amissio
Carcer
Tristitia

Court:
RW: Caput Draconis
LW: Tristitia
Judge: Conjunctio

Conjunctio as the judge seems like a "Yes". Caput Draconis as the RW seems to represent me in the state of "beginning", a lot of changes have been happening in my life but I feel ready to just begin. Tristitia might represent her being sad about something?

Reconciler: Fortuna Major

Maybe it means that it will turn out well if I contact her again?

Again, I went with the Shield Chart primarily, but I did notice that I, the Querent, as Acquisitio, differed from the Quesited in the 7th, Rubeus, by an active Earth. Could this mean that in some way, bringing some Earth energy to the situation might help bring about perfection?

Thank you

replies.title: Re: Geomancy readings
replies.content: You need to decide in advance whether you're doing a shield chart or a house chart, and stick with that -- otherwise you're at risk of cherrypicking.
